3091	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3092	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3093	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3094	  \mathlibok
3095	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3096	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3097	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3098	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3099	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3100	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3101	  \[
3102	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3103	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3104	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3105	  \]
3106	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3107	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3108	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3109	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3110	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3111	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3112	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3113	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3114	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3115	\end{lemma}

3128	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf condition as an equalizer of products]
3129	  \label{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}
3130	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts}
3131	  \mathlibok
3132	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf with values in a category with products on a
3134	  topological space, and let \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of
3135	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf
3136	  condition on \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if the fork
3137	  \[
3138	    \mathcal{F}(U)
3139	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
3140	    \prod_i \mathcal{F}(U_i)
3141	    \;\underset{\mathrm{rightRes}}{\overset{\mathrm{leftRes}}{\rightrightarrows}}\;
3142	    \prod_{(i,j)} \mathcal{F}(U_i \cap U_j)
3143	  \]
3144	  is a limit (an equalizer), where the two parallel maps restrict the \(U_i\)- and
3145	  \(U_j\)-components to \(U_i \cap U_j\). This is Mathlib's
3146	  \texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafEqualizerProducts} together with the fork
3147	  \texttt{SheafConditionEqualizerProducts.fork}; it is exactly Čech degree
3148	  \(0 \to 1\) and requires no separatedness.

3151	\begin{lemma}[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3152	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3154	  \uses{}
3155	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3156	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3157	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3158	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3159	  \begin{proof}
3160	    \uses{}
3161	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3162	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (Mathlib's
3163	    \texttt{Scheme.affineCover} together with the finite-subcover property of a
3164	    quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the intersection of two
3165	    quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is quasi-compact.
3166	    When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence the
3167	    intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib's
3168	    \texttt{IsSeparated}/\texttt{isAffineOpen\_inter} for separated schemes).
3169	  \end{proof}
3170	\end{lemma}

3178	  of \(X\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}. Then the module of global
3179	  sections is the equalizer of the two restriction maps of the cover,
3180	  \[

3234	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3235	    \qquad
3236	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3237	  \]
3238	  intertwining the two restriction maps, so that the whole diagram
3239	  \(\prod_i \Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3240	  \Gamma((U_{ij})_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to
3241	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3242	  \Gamma(U_{ij},\mathcal{F})\).
3243	  \begin{proof}
3244	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3245	    Base change preserves affineness and open immersions, so \((U_i)_B\) is affine and
3246	    \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) covers \(X_B\); since \(X\) is separated each \(U_{ij}\), hence each
3247	    \((U_{ij})_B\), is affine, and the fork of
3248	    Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} applies to \((X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). On each
3249	    affine term the global-sections form of the affine lemma
3250	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} supplies the isomorphism
3251	    \(\Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \cong \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\)
3252	    (and likewise on the \(U_{ij}\)); these are natural in restriction, so they
3253	    intertwine \(\mathrm{leftRes}\) and \(\mathrm{rightRes}\). Finally
3254	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)
3255	    (Mathlib's \texttt{TensorProduct.prodRight}, iterated), so the base-changed fork is
3256	    literally \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to the original fork.
3257	  \end{proof}
3258	\end{lemma}

3260	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, separated case]
3261	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_separated}
3262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso_of_isSeparated}
3263	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3264	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3265	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3266	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3267	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3268	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on
3269	  % taking cohomology."
3270	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3271	  separated paragraph.}
3272	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a separated, quasi-compact scheme over \(A\), and
3273	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3274	  \[
3275	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\longrightarrow\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3276	  \]
3277	  is an isomorphism.
3278	  \begin{proof}
3279	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3280	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3281	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}, \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) and
3282	    \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) are the equalizers of the finite forks of the cover
3283	    \(\mathcal{U}\) and its base change \(\mathcal{U}_B\). By
3284	    Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram} the \(X_B\)-fork is the
3285	    \(X\)-fork with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied. Since \(A \to B\) is flat,
3286	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
3287	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); hence
3288	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
3289	    \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B) = \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\),
3290	    and this iso is the comparison map.
3291	  \end{proof}
3292	\end{lemma}

3294	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, Mayer--Vietoris reduction of the quasi-separated case]
3295	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_mayerVietoris}
3297	  \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3298	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3299	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), quasi-separated paragraph
3300	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3301	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3302	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open
3303	  % covering $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. ... The reader who
3304	  % wishes to avoid this spectral sequence can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction
3305	  % on $t$ as in the proof of Lemma
3306	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
3307	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3308	  quasi-separated paragraph (Mayer--Vietoris induction on \(t\)).}
3309	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme over \(A\),
3310	  and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3311	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3312	  isomorphism.
3313	  \begin{proof}
3314	    \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3315	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3316	    Choose a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\)
3317	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}) and induct on \(t\). For \(t = 1\),
3318	    \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the affine lemma
3319	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}. For \(t > 1\) put \(V = U_t\)
3320	    (affine) and \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (covered by \(t-1\) affines, hence
3321	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated). Their intersection \(W \cap V =
3322	    \bigcup_{i<t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the \(t-1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each
3323	    an open of the affine \(U_i\) and so quasi-compact and separated; thus \(W \cap V\)
3324	    is separated and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated
3325	    case Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}. The two-member cover
3326	    \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris equalizer
3327	    \[
3328	      0 \to \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \to
3329	        \Gamma(W,\mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V,\mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(W\cap V,\mathcal{F}),
3330	    \]
3331	    and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness makes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3332	    preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
3333	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
3334	    \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t-1\) members), and for
3335	    \(W \cap V\) (separated), the five-lemma/equalizer comparison forces it for \(X\).
3336	  \end{proof}
3337	\end{lemma}

3339	\begin{lemma}[Reduction of the base-change map to the global-sections comparison]
3340	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}
3341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_isIso_iff_gammaTensorComparison}
3342	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
3343	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (the reduction "part (1) follows from
3344	  % part (2)") (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in
3345	  % the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3346	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent ..., it is the
3347	  % quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the $A$-module
3348	  % $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$ ... Since pullback by $g$
3349	  % corresponds to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules ... we see that it suffices to
3350	  % prove (2)."
3351	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the reduction
3352	  ``part (1) follows from part (2)''.}
3353	  In the cartesian square with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
3354	  quasi-separated, the sheaf-level base-change map
3355	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F}) \to f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of
3356	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3357	  after reducing to the affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\),
3358	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\), the module comparison map
3359	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3360	  isomorphism.
3361	  \begin{proof}
3362	    \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
3363	    Being an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we may take \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\)
3364	    and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
3365	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\)
3366	    (Stacks Tag 01XJ / Mathlib's quasi-coherence of the pushforward under a
3367	    quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism), hence is the sheaf associated to the
3368	    \(A\)-module \(\Gamma(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\); likewise
3369	    \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). Pullback along
3370	    \(g\) corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, so under the
3371	    tilde-equivalence the sheaf base-change map is the tilde of the comparison map
3372	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\); since
3373	    \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful on quasi-coherent modules, one is an
3374	    isomorphism iff the other is.
3375	  \end{proof}
3376	\end{lemma}

3423	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3424	  \[

3516	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3517	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3518	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3519	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3520	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.

3522	  \medskip
3523	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
3524	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
3525	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3526	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3527	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the module comparison map
3528	  \[

3530	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3531	  \]
3532	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
3533	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
3534	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
3535	
3536	  \medskip
3537	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
3538	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
3539	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
3540	  \[
3541	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3542	  \]
3543	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
3544	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
3545	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
3546	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3547	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3548	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
3549	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
3550	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
3551	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
3552	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
3553	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
3554	\end{proof}

2837	  \mathlibok
2838	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Localization}).}
2839	  If \(S\) is a localization of a commutative ring \(R\) at a multiplicative submonoid, then \(S\) is
2840	  a flat \(R\)-module. Consequently localization is exact and commutes with finite limits; this is
2841	  what lets the section-localization functor pass through the finite sheaf equalizer in the descent
2842	  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}).
2843	\end{lemma}
2844	
2845	% --- The finite basic-open cover (topological precursor) ---
2846	
2847	\begin{lemma}
2848	
2849	\leanok
2850	[Finite basic-open cover refining a quasi-coherence cover]
2851	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2853	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2854	  \textit{The topological finite-cover front of the gap1 transport: any quasi-coherence cover refines
2855	  to a finite basic-open cover.}
2856	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and let \(q\) be quasi-coherence data for \(M\)
2857	  (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a — possibly infinite — open cover
2858	  \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(\top\) together with a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)\) on each
2859	  member. Then there is a \emph{finite} subset \(t \subseteq R\) whose basic opens cover \(\Spec R\)
2860	  (equivalently \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\)) such that each \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), is contained in some
2861	  member \(q.X_i\) of the presentation cover.
2862	\end{lemma}
2863	\begin{proof}
2864	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2865	  The set \(G = \{\,r \in R : D(r) \le q.X_i \text{ for some } i\,\}\) spans the unit ideal. Indeed
2866	  every prime of \(R\) lies in some member \(q.X_i\) by the covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\)
2867	  (read off the \(\mathrm{Opens}\) Grothendieck-topology sieve), and the basic opens form a basis of
2868	  the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}), so a basic open \(D(r) \subseteq
2869	  q.X_i\) is a neighbourhood of that prime; hence \(\bigsqcup_{r \in G} D(r) = \top\), which is to say
2870	  \(\mathrm{span}\,G = (1)\). A unit-ideal membership uses only finitely many of the generators,
2871	  yielding a finite \(t \subseteq G\) with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\) and each \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for
2872	  \(r \in t\).
2873	\end{proof}

2878	% TODO of Mathlib's `Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean`: the equivalence of sheaf categories induced by
2879	% the site equivalence `Opens.overEquivalence U`. Together with the three Mathlib dependency
2880	% anchors below they form the topos-theoretic (purely topological) layer of bridge C

2884	\begin{lemma}[Slice of opens equivalent to opens of the open subspace]
2885	  \label{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2886	  \lean{TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence}
2887	  \mathlibok
2888	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over}).}
2889	  For a topological space \(X\) and an open \(U \subseteq X\), the slice category of opens of
2890	  \(X\) over \(U\) (the opens of \(X\) contained in \(U\), with inclusions) is equivalent to the
2891	  opens of the subspace \(U\):
2892	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)_{/U} \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U)\). Mathlib supplies this underlying
2893	  equivalence of sites; it leaves the lift to sheaf categories as an explicit TODO.
2894	\end{lemma}
2895	
2896	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf categories of equivalent sites are equivalent]
2897	  \label{lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib}
2898	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Equivalence.sheafCongr}
2899	  \mathlibok
2900	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Equivalence}).}
2901	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of categories carrying Grothendieck topologies \(J\)
2902	  on \(C\) and \(K\) on \(D\), and suppose \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) is a dense subsite for
2903	  \((K, J)\). Then for any target category \(A\) the sheaf categories are equivalent:
2904	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J, A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(K, A)\).
2905	\end{lemma}
2906	
2907	\begin{lemma}[Module sheaves transport across an equivalence of ringed sites]
2908	  \label{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}
2909	  \lean{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}
2910	  \mathlibok
2911	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2912	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PushforwardContinuous}).}
2913	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of sites with both \(e.\mathrm{functor}\) and
2914	  \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) continuous, and let \(S\) (a sheaf of rings on \(J\)) and \(R\) (a sheaf
2915	  of rings on \(K\)) be related by mutually inverse comparison maps across the continuous
2916	  pushforwards. Then the categories of sheaves of modules are equivalent,
2917	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,R \simeq \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,S\), compatibly with the
2918	  underlying ring-sheaf identification. This is the module-level lift used in step~3 of
2919	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}.
2920	\end{lemma}
2921	
2922	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2923	[The over-equivalence functor is cocontinuous]
2924	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2925	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2926	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2927	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
2928	  The functor of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib})
2929	  is cocontinuous (cover-lifting) from the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\) of the opens Grothendieck
2930	  topology of \(X\) to the opens Grothendieck topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\).
2931	\end{lemma}
2932	\begin{proof}
2933	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2934	  A sieve covers in the slice topology \(J_X|_U\) iff its image sieve covers in \(J_X\), and
2935	  covering for the opens topology is pointwise: a sieve covers iff its members' opens cover the
2936	  base set-theoretically. Given a point \(x\) of a slice object and a cover member in \(J_U\)
2937	  containing the image of \(x\), transport it across the open embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
2938	  which is injective: the set-theoretic image of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is an open of \(X\)
2939	  contained in the base, a neighbourhood of \(x\) lying in the image sieve. This witnesses the
2940	  cover-lifting condition.
2941	\end{proof}
2942	
2943	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2944	[The over-equivalence inverse is cocontinuous]
2945	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2946	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2947	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2948	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
2949	  Symmetrically, the inverse of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) is cocontinuous from the
2950	  opens topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\) to the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\).
2951	\end{lemma}
2952	\begin{proof}
2953	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2954	  As in \cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}, reduce to the pointwise covering
2955	  criterion and the over-sieve image criterion; given a point and a slice-cover member, pull the
2956	  member back along the embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\) (preimage of an open of \(X\) is an open
2957	  of the subspace) to obtain a cover member of \(J_U\) witnessing the lift.
2958	\end{proof}

2960	\begin{lemma}\leanok

2962	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}

2985	\begin{lemma}\leanok

2996	\begin{definition}\leanok
2997	[The over-site/open-subspace sheaf equivalence]
2998	  \label{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}
2999	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_sheafCongr}
3000	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib,
3001	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}
3002	  \textit{Keystone of the topological layer of bridge C.}
3003	  For any category \(A\), the site equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\)
3004	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}) lifts to an equivalence of sheaf categories
3005	  \[
3006	    \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\, A) \;\simeq\; \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\, A),
3007	  \]
3008	  obtained by feeding the dense-subsite witness
3009	  (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}) to the general sheaf-congruence
3010	  construction (\cref{lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib}). This is exactly the equivalence left
3011	  as a TODO in Mathlib's \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean}, and it is the object across which
3012	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso} transports the structure sheaf and the module \(M\).
3013	\end{definition}

3015	% --- (C, step 3) The module-level equivalence and its functor identification ---
3016	
3017	% The two blocks below are the module-category layer assembled on top of the topological
3018	% sheaf-congruence def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr: the step-3 equivalence of categories of sheaves of
3019	% modules (def:over_restrict_equiv) and the step-4 functor identification (lem:over_restrict_functor_iso)
3020	% whose object component is the slice-touching bridge lem:over_restrict_iso below. They are
3021	% project-bespoke infrastructure (no external source).
3022	
3023	\begin{definition}
3024	[The slice-to-geometric module-category equivalence (gap1, C, step 3)]
3025	  \label{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3026	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictEquiv}
3027	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3028	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3029	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3030	  \textit{Module-level lift of the topological bridge C; the step-3 equivalence on which the
3031	  slice-touching isomorphism is built.}
3032	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The category of sheaves of modules over the
3033	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) is
3034	  equivalent to the category \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}\) of sheaves of modules on the open
3035	  subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\):
3036	  \[
3037	    \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\bigr)
3038	      \;\simeq\; U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}.
3039	  \]
3040	  This is the module-category lift of the topological sheaf equivalence
3041	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}.
3042	\end{definition}
3043	\begin{proof}
3044	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3045	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3046	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3047	  Apply the module-sheaf transport \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3048	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}) to the site equivalence
3049	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), whose two legs are
3050	  both continuous (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3051	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}). The transport consumes a pair of mutually inverse
3052	  ring-sheaf comparison morphisms between the sliced structure sheaf
3053	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) and the geometric structure sheaf
3054	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U.\mathrm{toScheme}}\); for these one takes the whiskered (co)unit of the site
3055	  equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) into the structure presheaf, paired with the
3056	  identity. The identity is legal precisely because the geometric structure sheaf is
3057	  \emph{definitionally} the transport of the sliced one across the ring-valued instance of
3058	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr} (this is step~2 of the bridge, which holds by
3059	  definitional equality). Orienting the resulting equivalence so that its functor runs from the slice
3060	  side to the geometric side gives the stated equivalence.
3061	\end{proof}
3062	
3063	\begin{lemma}
3064	[Functor identification of the slice-to-geometric equivalence (gap1, C, step 4)]
3065	  \label{lem:over_restrict_functor_iso}
3066	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictFunctorIso}
3067	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3068	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3069	  \textit{The composite of the slice functor with the step-3 equivalence is the geometric restriction
3070	  functor.}
3071	  With \(X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) as above, the composite of the abstract Grothendieck-slice functor
3072	  \(M \mapsto M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) (pushforward along the identity of the sliced ring sheaf) with the
3073	  functor of the step-3 equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) is naturally isomorphic to the
3074	  geometric restriction functor along the open immersion \(U.\iota\):
3075	  \[
3076	    \bigl(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\mathbf{1}\bigr) \;\circ\; (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}
3077	      \;\cong\; \mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota
3078	  \]
3079	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}).
3080	\end{lemma}
3081	\begin{proof}
3082	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3083	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3084	  Both sides are pushforwards of sheaves of modules along the \emph{same} opens functor. Indeed the
3085	  opens functor of the open immersion factors as
3086	  \[
3087	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3088	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}\;\circ\;\mathrm{Over.forget}\,U
3089	  \]
3090	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), an equality that holds by definitional equality, and
3091	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely
3092	  pushforward along \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). The left-hand composite is pushforward along the
3093	  factored functor on the right. Identify the two via the composition law for module-sheaf
3094	  pushforward (\(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\)) followed by congruence of pushforward along the equal ring
3095	  morphisms (\(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)); the required ring-morphism equality is the routine
3096	  comparison discharged automatically by the category-theoretic discharger.
3097	\end{proof}
3098	
3099	% --- (C) The single slice-touching bridge ---
3100	
3101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3102	[The Grothendieck-slice restriction equals the geometric restriction (gap1, C)]
3103	  \label{lem:over_restrict_iso}
3104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso}
3105	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
3106	    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr,
3107	    lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous, lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous,
3108	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}
3109	  % NOTE (iter-031): RESOLVED and axiom-clean. AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso
3110	  % now exists (#print axioms = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}); gap1 bridge C
3111	  % (steps 1->4) is fully closed. The step-2 geometric ring-sheaf identification collapsed to
3112	  % `rfl` (U.toScheme.ringCatSheaf is definitionally the transport of the sliced sheaf, via
3113	  % toScheme_presheaf_obj/map). The statement IS routed through the step-3 equivalence functor as
3114	  % anticipated -- overRestrictIso : (overRestrictEquiv U).functor.obj (M.over U) ≅
3115	  % (restrictFunctor U.ι).obj M -- so no further signature sharpening is needed. The
3116	  % `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` incantation was NOT needed here;
3117	  % reassess only for the downstream P1 Presentation.map transport if its slice synthesis times out.
3118	  \textit{The one lemma that must touch the Grothendieck slice site; everything downstream of it is
3119	  geometric.}
3120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The \emph{abstract Grothendieck-slice}
3121	  restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) — an object of the category of sheaves of modules over the
3122	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) —
3123	  is canonically isomorphic to the \emph{geometric} restriction
3124	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\)
3125	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), equivalently to the pullback \(U.\iota^{*} M\)
3126	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), as a sheaf of modules on the small Zariski
3127	  site of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\). The isomorphism is induced by the equivalence
3128	  of sites \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U.\mathrm{toScheme})\) coming from the opens
3129	  functor of the open immersion \(U.\iota\), under which the sliced sheaf of rings
3130	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the structure sheaf of \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\).
3131	  For \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) a basic open, the target category is
3132	  \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\)
3133	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3134	\end{lemma}
3135	\begin{proof}
3136	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3137	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3138	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3139	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
3140	  The isomorphism is built in four steps, ascending from the purely topological layer to the
3141	  module-level statement.
3142	
3143	  \emph{Step 1 (topological layer --- done).} The opens functor of the open immersion
3144	  \(U.\iota\) realizes the slice of the opens site of \(X\) over \(U\) as the opens site of the
3145	  subspace \(U\); on sheaf categories this is the equivalence
3146	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\,A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\,A)\) of
3147	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}, for every target category \(A\). This is the
3148	  topos-theoretic layer, assembled from the cocontinuity/continuity of both legs of
3149	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}).
3150	
3151	  \emph{Step 2 (geometric ring-sheaf identification --- the current obstacle).} Identify the
3152	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) with the structure sheaf
3153	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U}\) of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\), transported across the
3154	  \(\mathrm{RingCat}\)-valued instance of \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}; this is an
3155	  isomorphism of sheaves of rings. The bridge is the factorization of the opens functor of the
3156	  immersion,
3157	  \[
3158	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3159	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}
3160	        \;\circ\; \mathrm{Over.forget}\,U ,
3161	  \]
3162	  under which the structure-sheaf comparison reduces to the standard open-immersion
3163	  structure-sheaf identifications; recall that \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\)
3164	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely pushforward along
3165	  \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). This ring-sheaf identification is the remaining geometric
3166	  obstacle.
3167	
3168	  \emph{Step 3 (lift to modules).} Given the step-2 identification of the underlying ring sheaves
3169	  and the continuity of both legs (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3170	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}), lift the sheaf-of-rings comparison to an
3171	  equivalence of categories of sheaves of modules via
3172	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3173	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}). Under this equivalence the sliced
3174	  module \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the geometric restriction
3175	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\).
3176	
3177	  \emph{Step 4 (compose).} Compose with
3178	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
3179	  to land the isomorphism \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U \cong M.\mathrm{restrict}\,U.\iota \cong
3180	  U.\iota^{*}M\). The two sides live a priori in different module categories, so the literal Lean
3181	  statement of \(\mathrm{overRestrictIso}\) is phrased through the step-3 equivalence functor; for
3182	  \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) the target is \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under
3183	  \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3184	\end{proof}

3186	% --- (C, step 4') The pullback form of the slice-to-geometric isomorphism ---
3187	
3188	\begin{lemma}
3189	[The slice-to-geometric isomorphism in pullback form (gap1, C, step 4')]
3190	  \label{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}
